title: Understanding Dog Excitement Urination: Tips and Triage Advice
meta_desc: Learn why dogs leak when excited, the urgency level for this behavior, and how to help your puppy manage their bladder during greetings.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excitement urination is an involuntary response in high-arousal moments common in puppies; while typically low urgency, you should see a vet if you notice blood, straining, or leaking during sleep.

What is excitement urination and why does it happen to my dog?

Excitement urination is an involuntary physical response where a dog, most commonly a puppy, loses bladder control during high-arousal moments. This typically happens during greetings, intense play, or when someone enters the home. Unlike a potty training lapse, the dog isn't choosing to go; their nervous system simply overrides their bladder control temporarily.

Is my dog's excitement urination a medical emergency?

The urgency level for excitement urination is Low. While this is usually a behavioral or developmental stage that puppies outgrow, you should schedule a non-emergency appointment if you notice:

  • Straining to urinate
  • Blood in the urine
  • Leaking that occurs while the dog is sleeping
  • A need to rule out medical issues like infections or ectopic ureters

How can a photo or video help my veterinarian triage my dog?

Providing visual evidence can help your clinical team determine the next steps more accurately:

  • Photo of the urine: Capturing this on a light-colored surface allows the team to assess the color and concentration.
  • Video of the behavior: Seeing the event helps distinguish between excitement urination and submissive urination, which require different management strategies.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excitement urination in dogs often occurs during greetings or when showing submissive postures. Treatment involves avoiding stimuli that incite the behavior, such as direct approach or eye contact, and refraining from punishment during greeting to avoid increasing fear and conflict. Alternative behaviors incompatible with excitable greetings, such as a relaxed sit or a learned trick, can be taught. Phenylpropanolamine may increase sphincter control, while imipramine can improve control and reduce anxiety.

Chapter: Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1569)

faqs.question: Will my puppy grow out of excitement urination?
faqs.answer: Yes, most puppies grow out of this behavior by the time they are one year old as they develop better physical bladder control and emotional maturity.

faqs.question: How should I react when my dog leaks?
faqs.answer: It is important not to punish or scold your dog, as this can lead to anxiety and worsen the problem. Simply clean it up without a fuss and try to keep future greetings very calm.

faqs.question: Can medication help with this behavior?
faqs.answer: While usually managed through behavior modification, in some chronic adult cases, veterinarians may prescribe medications to increase urethral sphincter tone if a medical cause is contributing.
